To appear by Zoom, go to zoom.us and click "Join Meeting." Enter Meeting ID number 1602254765 and passcode 514879. To appear telephonically through Zoom, call 1-669-900-6833 and follow the prompts. rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE THE CHUNG HOP WONG AND KING TSUI LAU WONG REVOCABL TRUST dated sep 21, 2009 amended Sep 26 2009
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion For Order Quashing Deposition Subpoena For Production Of Business Records And For Expenses Of The Motion
rulings.Rulings: [Part 1 of 2] This tentative ruling is issued by Judge Ross C. Moody. Before the court is Petitioners Brandon Wong's, Jessica Wong's, and Helen Wong-Joe's Motion for Order Quashing Deposition Subpoena for Production of Business Records and for Expenses of the Motion. Per the Notice of Motion, Petitioners seek an order pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure section 1987.1, quashing the May 28, 2025 deposition subpoena for production of business records serve on JP Morgan Chase bank, for failure to comply with Code of Civil Procedure section 1985, subdivisions (b), and (d) and due to the subpoena constituting an unreasonable and oppressive demand for documents. Per the Errata to the Notice of Motion, Petitioners further seek $2,900 in sanctions against opposing counsel for failure to meet and confer in good faith. The Motion is GRANTED, as follows: Under CCP sec. 1985.3(b), the consumer whose personal records are sought must be served a copy of the subpoena, the Notice to Consumer form (SUBP-025), and "proof of service as indicated in paragraph (1) of subdivision (c)" of section 1985.3. Under Code of Civil Procedure section 2020.410(c), "A deposition subpoena that commands only the production of business records for copying need not be accompanied by an affidavit or declaration showing good cause for the production of the business records designated in it." Service of the required documents may be either personally, or at the consumer's last known address, or in accordance with Code of Civil Procedure section 1010 et seq., or, if the consumer is a party, to the attorney of record. This service must occur at least five days prior to service on the custodian of records, and not less than ten days prior to the date of production specified in the subpoena, plus any additional time required for service by mail under Section 1013. Here, the Deposition Subpoena for Production of Records (form SUBP-010) directed to JPMorgan Chase Bank was issued May 28, 2025, with a production date of June 26, 2025. The Notice to Consumer (form SUBP-025) directed to Brandon Wong and Vivien Chau is also dated May 28, 2025, with proof of service by mail on the same date. Thus, the Notice to Consumer was not served at least five days prior to service on the custodian of records, as required under Section 1985.3(b). The Motion argues for an additional finding pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure section 1987.1 that the subpoena at issue "contains unreasonable and oppressive demands for documents." The court does not make such a finding on the basis of the instant record and grants the Motion to Quash on the grounds discussed above. However, the court notes, a right of privacy does exist as to a party's confidential financial affairs (see, e.g., Fortunato v. Sup. Ct. (2003) 114 Cal. App. 4th 475, 481). "[A] court faced with a motion for protective order should carefully balance "the right of civil litigants to discover relevant facts, on the one hand, with the right of bank customers to maintain reasonable privacy regarding their financial affairs, on the other." (Valley Bank of Nevada v. Superior Ct. (1975) 15 Cal. 3d 652, 656.) Among other considerations, the party seeking discovery of confidential information has the burden of making a threshold showing that the evidence sought is "directly relevant" to the claim or defense. The broad "relevant to the subject matter" standard under Code of Civil Procedure section 2017.010 and Evidence Code section 210 is insufficient. (Harris v. Superior Ct. (1992) 3 Cal. App. 4th 661, 665 (disapproved on other grounds by Williams v. Sup.Ct. (Marshalls of CA, LLC) (2017) 3 C5th 531, 557 & fn. 8); see also, Valley Bank, supra, at p. 658, and Fortunato, supra, at p. 481.) The court, in its discretion under Code of Civil Procedure section 1987.2, DENIES the request for award of sanctions.
